did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically five state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks (CNNs) trained with transfer learning, to classify skin cancer. Experiments were conducted on the HAM10000 dataset to differentiate between seven species of moles, with DenseNet201 identified as the best performing model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of five state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks (CNNs) using transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin cancer. It includes a methodology section, data analysis, and specific performance metrics (accuracy, F1 score, etc.), which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Prediction using Machine Learning and Neural Networks
authors: Mr. Dilip Kumar Jang Bahadur Saini
journal: 2022 5th International Conference on Contemporary Computing and Informatics (IC3I)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer diagnosis by developing a deep learning system to classify seven different species of moles using the HAM10000 dataset. Five state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks were trained via transfer learning, with data augmentation techniques applied to mitigate class imbalance. The DenseNet201 architecture emerged as the best-performing model, achieving high classification accuracies and F-measures with very few false negatives. The research also compared a simple binary classification model against a hierarchical two-stage approach, finding the simple model superior. The results suggest that this automated classification method can effectively assist medical practitioners in detecting and categorizing skin cancer types.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven distinct classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.

One of the seven distinct classes in the HAM10000 dataset; noted as having over 7000 photos, creating class imbalance. Also used as a binary class ('nevi') in a two-stage classification model.

all_task_types.target: Seven different species of moles (actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, nevi, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, vascular skin) and binary classification between nevi and non-nevi.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses deep learning (CNNs) and machine learning (SVM, NN) to classify skin cancer types. Specifically, it performs multiclass classification on the HAM10000 dataset with seven classes and a hierarchical two-stage approach involving binary classification (nevi vs. non-nevi). The primary task is discrete diagnostic categorization.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of dermoscopic images, also known as the Human Against Machine with 10000 training images dataset. It was gathered over more than 20 years from the Dermatology Department at the Medical University of Vienna (Austria) and the Skin Cancer Clinic of Cliff Rosendahl in Queensland (Australia). The dataset is publicly available and widely used as a baseline for comparing human and automated skin cancer classification.

use.training: Used for training five state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks (CNNs) via transfer learning. Data augmentation (horizontal/vertical flipping, rotations) was applied to balance the skewed class distribution, particularly for the underrepresented classes.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the classification models. The paper notes that the test set performance must be carefully assessed due to the skewed distribution in the training stage.
use.other: Used as a reference standard for dermatology trials and for comparing the proposed deep learning models against human specialists and other robots.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To mitigate class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by applying horizontal/vertical flipping and rotations to the training images.
To detect main causes related to skin cancer and analyze the relationship between predictor variables and the dependent variable.
To assess the performance of the Neural Network model by measuring the average squared difference between estimated and actual values.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It relied on performance metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, precision, recall, and error measures (MSE, MAE, RMSE) to evaluate the models.

HAM10000.experiment: Multiclass Classification (7 classes) using Transfer Learning
HAM10000.notes: The DenseNet201 network performed well with high classification accuracies and F-measures. The simple model (binary classification between nevi and non-nevi) outperformed the 2-levels hierarchical model, with the best result coming from the first level. The NN model had an accuracy of 0.978, precision of 0.962, recall of 1.0, and F1 score of 0.981. The SVM model used a Gaussian kernel with specific hyperparameters.

architecture_used.main_model: Five state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks (CNNs) including DenseNet201, Inception-ResNetV2, MobileNetV2, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and a custom Neural Network (NN). DenseNet201 was identified as the best performing model.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used to retrain existing effective models. Specific pretraining datasets mentioned for context include ImageNet, COCO, and VOC, though the paper primarily notes the use of transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset.

architecture_details.DenseNet201: Identified as the best performing model with high classification accuracies and F-measures.
architecture_details.Inception-ResNetV2: A mix of two deep networks aiming to gain from ResNet's residual connections by speeding up Inception network training.
architecture_details.MobileNetV2: A transportable neural network geared for resource-constrained contexts, consisting of 53 layers, utilizing inverted residual and linear bottleneck.
architecture_details.Support Vector Machine (SVM): Used a Gaussian kernel. Hyperparameters included learning algorithm 0.1, training iterations 100, learning base weighting 0.1, and min-max normalizer.
architecture_details.Neural Network (NN): A two-class unit consisting of 13 inputs, 1 convolution layer, and 1 output. Used a hidden layer with an MSE of 0.2675.

data_augmentation: Rotations of images with a frequency of 0.75 and an arbitrary angle
training_details.optimization: Not explicitly reported for CNNs. For NN, Mean Squared Error (MSE) was used as the error metric. For SVM, a Gaussian kernel was used.
training_details.loss_function: Mean Squared Error (MSE) for the Neural Network model.

classification_or_output_head.task: Multiclass classification (7 classes) and binary classification (nevi vs. non-nevi).
classification_or_output_head.output: Probability of skin cancer (closer to 1 indicates higher likelihood).
classification_or_output_head.metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 score, F-measure, Sensitivity, Specificity, True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, False Negatives.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to mitigate class imbalance: horizontal flipping (probability 0.5), vertical flipping (probability 0.5), and rotations (frequency 0.75, arbitrary angle between [-90, 90])
Min-max normalization applied to SVM model inputs

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a frequency of 0.75 and an arbitrary angle between [-90, 90] degrees.

conclusions: Deep learning systems, particularly those using convolutional neural networks (CNNs), can enhance skin cancer diagnosis or perform on par with human specialists.
The DenseNet201 network performed well in the experiment, achieving high classification accuracies and F-measures with very few false negatives.
A simple binary classification model (nevi vs. non-nevi) outperformed the hierarchical two-levels model, with the best results coming from the first level.
The Neural Network (NN) model achieved an accuracy of 0.978, precision of 0.962, recall of 1.0, and an F1 score of 0.981.
The SVM model utilized a Gaussian kernel with a learning algorithm of 0.1, 100 training iterations, and min-max normalization.

Data augmentation (horizontal/vertical flipping and rotations) was necessary to mitigate the class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, where the nevi class had over 7000 photos compared to approximately 1000 for other classes.
AI is making headway in dermatology and can revolutionize clinical outcomes by improving the sensitivity and precision of detection for skin lesions.
Medical and photographic datasets of all skin types are needed for AI research, requiring increased global skin imaging coordination.
AI is not a danger to dermatologists' knowledge but may be employed to enhance clinical practice in the next decades.
important_remarks: The study relies on the HAM10000 dataset, which has a significant class imbalance (nevi class has >7000 images vs. ~1000 for others), a flaw the authors acknowledge but note data augmentation cannot fully eliminate.

The paper highlights a critical limitation in the field: lack of robust out-of-sample (OOS) or out-of-distribution (OOD) testing for skin cancer classifiers, noting that current datasets may not adequately test classifier robustness to real-world variations.
The authors warn that CNNs may learn 'shortcut learning' (erroneous correlations/artifacts) rather than reliable physical features, leading to poor generalization on unseen data.

The simple binary classification model (nevi vs. non-nevi) outperformed the hierarchical two-stage model, suggesting that complex hierarchical approaches may not be necessary or effective for this specific task.
The paper does not report exact accuracy values for the DenseNet201 model, only describing them as 'high', which limits the ability to compare its performance quantitatively against other models.

The study is a conference paper with limited scope; it acknowledges that medical and photographic datasets of all skin types are needed for future AI research, indicating current data diversity issues.

Deployment concerns are noted, including the need for hypersensitivity, specificity, and effectiveness recording in actual clinical environments, and hurdles like protecting health information and retraining algorithms.

The Neural Network model reported an accuracy of 0.978 and F1 score of 0.981, but the Support Vector Machine (SVM) model's performance metrics are not explicitly provided in the results section, only its hyperparameters.
